principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal has conclusively determined that the sedition offence under section 10 of the Crimes Ordinance constitutes an offence endangering national security.

principles.principle_label: Mandatory designated judge for national security cases
principles.paraphrase_en: For cases concerning offences endangering national security, the National Security Law mandates that bail applications and trials must be presided over by a designated judge.

An offence classified as endangering national security at the bail stage must retain that same classification throughout the trial proceedings; inconsistent classification within the same case is not permissible.

principles.principle_label: Proper procedure for listing before designated judge
principles.paraphrase_en: It is proper procedure for the prosecution to request that a case involving a national security offence be listed before a designated judge, as required by law.

principles.principle_label: Request for designated judge not judge-shopping
principles.paraphrase_en: A prosecution request for a case to be heard by a designated judge, as mandated for national security offences, does not constitute improper judge-shopping when no particular judge is specified.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the CFA's conclusive determination that sedition under section 10 of the Crimes Ordinance is an offence endangering national security.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG HAU YI SIDNEY (伍巧怡)
judges: Appeal Committee: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ and Mr Justice Fok PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The more stringent bail threshold under Article 42(2) of the National Security Law applies not only to offences created by the NSL itself, but also to acts endangering national security that are capable of constituting an offence under the NSL or under other HKSAR laws that safeguard national security.

The phrase 'acts endangering national security' in NSL Article 42(2) should be construed broadly to cover acts capable of constituting an offence under the National Security Law or under other HKSAR laws enacted to safeguard national security.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the construction of NSL Article 42(2) established in the Lai case.

judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, there must be provoking conduct that caused the defendant to lose self-control. If no such provocation exists, the defense fails and the defendant is guilty of murder.

When assessing provocation, the jury must consider whether a person of ordinary self-control would have reacted as the accused did, taking into account the accused's entire factual situation including their history, experiences, background, and personal attributes.
The law of provocation should not indirectly incorporate the concept of diminished responsibility, as these are distinct legal defenses with different requirements and purposes.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lord Simon's approach in Camplin regarding consideration of the 'entire factual situation' when assessing provocation.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Barnes J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of electronic messages as recent complaint
principles.paraphrase_en: Electronic messages, such as WhatsApp messages, can be admissible as recent complaint evidence even if the recipient of those messages does not testify in court.

The requirement for a complaint to be 'recent' involves not only a temporal assessment but also a broader contextual and subjective evaluation, considering factors like the nature of the assault, the relationship with the assailant, and the complainant's characteristics.

For a communication to qualify as a complaint of a sexual offence, it must at least convey to the recipient that a sexual wrong by the accused is being alleged, though it need not recite all the legal ingredients of the offence.

When determining if conduct qualifies as a complaint, the court must consider the whole circumstances holistically; factors like consistency with the later account can be part of this assessment and need not be considered in rigid isolation.

An appellate court should be slow to overturn a verdict based on a challenge to tactical decisions made by trial counsel, unless there are exceptional circumstances or a demonstrable miscarriage of justice.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that WhatsApp messages can be admissible as recent complaint evidence even if the recipient does not testify.
The judgment applies the modern legal guidance from this case on the contextual factors for determining what constitutes a 'recent' complaint.
The judgment cites this case alongside Chak Kong Fai for guidance on the law concerning recent complaints in sexual offence cases.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that appellate courts should be reluctant to interfere with verdicts based on trial counsel's tactical decisions.
The judgment applies the established principle from this case concerning appellate restraint regarding trial counsel's tactical choices.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. LAI CHEE YING
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Appeal by Case Stated vs rehearing
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under Section 105 of the Magistrates Ordinance (by way of Case Stated) is distinct from an appeal under Section 113 (by way of rehearing). The appellate court in a Case Stated appeal does not reassess the weight of trial evidence, including witness testimony; it is a review.

A magistrate's finding of fact constitutes an error of law (perversity) if no reasonable magistrate, properly directing themselves, could have reached that conclusion. This requires showing the magistrate misdirected themselves, misunderstood facts, considered irrelevant matters, or overlooked relevant ones.

For criminal intimidation, the key questions are the intention behind the threat and its effect (or likely effect) on the recipient. This is assessed by reference to the specific circumstances of both persons involved at the time the words were spoken, regardless of whether the threat was made directly, reported later, or not reported at all.

The court must consider whether a threat is of a kind likely to overcome the ordinary free will of a person of ordinary firmness. The actual alarm of the recipient is a relevant factor, but the test is objective (the 'firm man'), not purely subjective.
A suspect's denial or rejection of a proposition during a police interview cannot, without more, be treated as an admission or confession of that same proposition.

relationships.target_case_name: Lo Tong Kai v The Queen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the legal principle from Lo Tong Kai regarding the relevant questions for criminal intimidation (intention and effect of the threat).
The judgment cites Kwan Sing Kit as a comparable case where the parties had a long history of conflict, which is relevant to assessing the context of the threat.

cross_references.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. DINH KA YAN, GIGI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court
candidates.topic_label: Trial Evidence and Burden of Proof

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factual basis after jury verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing judge must determine the factual basis for sentence consistently with the jury's verdict. If the verdict has only one possible interpretation, the judge must sentence on that basis. If multiple interpretations are possible, the judge must determine the facts for sentencing by applying the criminal standard of proof to the trial evidence.
A judge is entitled to form an independent view of the facts relevant to sentencing, provided that view is not inconsistent or in conflict with the jury's verdict.
A party cannot invite the court to go behind or undermine the jury's verdict when establishing the factual basis for sentencing.

relationships.description: The court endorses the general statements of principle made by Stock JA in that case regarding sentencing after a jury verdict.
